title: SEGMENT 17 — Oncoretroviruses and the birth of the oncogene concept

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Oncoretroviruses and the birth of the oncogene concept
I want to take one more historical detour, because it connects to something foundational in cancer biology that grew directly out of retrovirology.
Recall Peyton Rous and his sarcoma virus.
RSV transforms infected cells into tumor cells.
In the 1970s, the question was: how?
What does this virus carry that makes a cell cancerous?
The viral genome was sequenced and analyzed.
RSV, unlike most retroviruses, has gag, pol, env — and a fourth gene called v-src, for viral sarcoma.
v-src was the transforming gene.
Delete v-src and the virus still replicates but no longer transforms.
In 1976, Harold Varmus and Michael Bishop, at UCSF, asked: where did v-src come from?
They used radioactive v-src DNA as a probe and went hunting through normal, uninfected chicken cells.
To everyone's astonishment, they found a closely related gene already present in the chicken genome.
They called it c-src, for cellular src.
It was a normal cellular gene.
v-src was the retrovirus's hijacked, slightly mutated version of a perfectly normal cellular gene.
This was a paradigm shift.
It meant that the transforming gene of a retrovirus was not, in some fundamental sense, a viral invention.
The virus had picked up a host gene at some point in its evolutionary history, mutated it, and was now wielding it against the host.
More than that — it meant the host genome already contained genes whose mutated forms could cause cancer.
These genes were named proto-oncogenes.
Their mutated, hyperactive versions were named oncogenes.
Varmus and Bishop won the Nobel Prize in 1989.

The concept of cellular oncogenes — that cancer is driven, in significant part, by mutations in our own genes that knock them into the "on" position — became one of the central organizing principles of modern cancer biology.

We now know dozens of proto-oncogenes — RAS, MYC, ERBB, EGFR, FOS, JUN, ABL, and many more — and many of them were originally identified through their viral counterparts in transforming retroviruses isolated from various animal cancers.
It's worth pausing on this.
The provirus hypothesis from segment 2 rewrote the central dogma.
The discovery of cellular oncogenes from acute transforming retroviruses rewrote our understanding of cancer.
Retroviruses, this tiny family, have had an outsized influence on the foundations of molecular biology.
Now, in humans, the situation is rather different.
There are very few human oncoretroviruses.
The main one — and as of today, the only firmly established directly-oncogenic human retrovirus — is HTLV-1, human T-lymphotropic virus 1.
HTLV-1 infects about 10 to 20 million people worldwide, with hotspots in southern Japan, the Caribbean, parts of sub-Saharan Africa, parts of South America, and Aboriginal Australia.
Most HTLV-1 infections are lifelong but asymptomatic.
About 2 to 5 percent of infected individuals develop adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma, ATL, a usually fatal mature T-cell malignancy.

Another small fraction develop a chronic neuroinflammatory disease called tropical spastic paraparesis or HTLV-1-associated myelopathy, HAM/TSP, in which the virus drives CD8 T cell infiltration of the spinal cord.
HTLV-1's oncogenic mechanism is different from RSV's.
HTLV-1 doesn't carry a hijacked cellular oncogene.
Instead, it encodes its own regulatory proteins — Tax and HBZ — that disrupt host cell-cycle control, activate NF-κB, and accumulate genetic damage in infected CD4 T cells over years and decades.
The other human retrovirus you'll hear about is HTLV-2, but it has no firmly established disease association.

HIV is not directly oncogenic in the way HTLV-1 is, but immunosuppression from HIV does enable a cluster of cancers — Kaposi sarcoma, certain lymphomas, cervical cancer — driven by other viruses or by loss of immune surveillance.

So, in the human disease space: HTLV-1 is the rare directly-transforming retrovirus, HIV is the dominant disease-causing retrovirus, and a handful of others may have minor or yet-to-be-confirmed roles.
